attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old man visits his GP to request screening for bowel cancer. He has no current symptoms of concern, His brother was recently diagnosed with small intestinal cancer at age 37, his sister died at age 41 from pancreatic cancer shortly after treatment for ovarian cancer, and his father was diagnosed with colorectal cancer at age 45.

The patient has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He works as a groundskeeper, is a non-smoker and maintains a healthy diet. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Two-yearly immunochemical faecal occult blood testing
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a striking family history suggestive of Lynch syndrome, with three first-degree relatives diagnosed with Lynch syndrome-associated cancers at young ages. Refer for genetic risk assessment is the most appropriate next step, as identifying a pathogenic variant would guide both the patient's surveillance program and testing of other family members.

Colonoscopy every five years would be inadequate surveillance if Lynch syndrome is confirmed, as these patients typically require annual or biennial colonoscopy starting at age 20-25. Additionally, colonoscopy alone would miss other Lynch syndrome-associated cancers that require different screening modalities. The surveillance interval and comprehensive screening program should be determined after genetic testing results are available.

Annual computed tomography colonography is not an appropriate surveillance tool for high-risk patients. It is primarily used when colonoscopy is incomplete or contraindicated, and exposes patients to unnecessary radiation. Furthermore, it cannot detect small polyps as effectively as colonoscopy and does not allow for immediate removal of suspicious lesions.

Two-yearly immunochemical faecal occult blood testing is appropriate for average-risk population screening from age 45, but is insufficient for someone with this family history suggestive of hereditary cancer syndrome. This testing modality has lower sensitivity than colonoscopy and would miss many of the cancers associated with Lynch syndrome.

Daily aspirin for chemoprevention may have a role in reducing cancer risk in confirmed Lynch syndrome, but should only be initiated after genetic diagnosis and under specialist guidance regarding optimal dosing and duration. Starting chemoprevention before confirming the diagnosis could expose the patient to unnecessary medication risks.

Lynch syndrome is an autosomal dominant condition caused by pathogenic variants in DNA mismatch repair genes. Associated cancers include colorectal, endometrial, ovarian, gastric, pancreatic, small bowel, urinary tract, and other specific malignancies. The diagnosis has important implications for cancer surveillance strategies in both the patient and their relatives.
learningPoints: Family history of multiple Lynch syndrome-related cancers in first-degree relatives under age 50 warrants urgent genetic risk assessment.
Lynch syndrome-related cancers include colorectal, endometrial, ovarian, gastric, pancreatic, small intestinal and other specific malignancies.
Genetic testing should precede decisions about surveillance intervals and chemoprevention in suspected hereditary cancer syndromes.
references: Early detection of cancers, Colorectal cancer. Royal Australian College of General Practitioners [Internet]. 2021 Dec 8;189–93. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/key-racgp-guidelines/view-all-racgp-guidelines/guidelines-for-preventive-activities-in-general-pr/early-detection-of-cancers/colorectal-cancer#ref-num-22

Burn J, Sheth H, Elliott F, Reed L, Macrae F, Mecklin JP, et al. Cancer prevention with aspirin in hereditary colorectal cancer (Lynch syndrome), 10-year follow-up and registry-based 20-year data in the CAPP2 study: a double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled trial. The Lancet [Internet]. 2020 Jun 13;395(10240):1855–63. Available from: https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(20)30366-4/fulltext
